soap:
REVIEW · lancedb/lancedb#3489 — feat(query): add use_lsm_read to query data via the MemWAL LSM scanner
touch-of-grey (regular) · XL (+1135/-0, 16 files)  ⚠ escalation: public-api + large-blast-radius

ONE-LINER
  Adds an opt-in `use_lsm_read` query flag: when a table has an LSM write spec, reads route
  through Lance's MemWAL `LsmScanner`/LSM planners so in-flight `merge_insert` data (active +
  frozen memtables and flushed L0 generations) is visible, deduplicated by primary key
  (newest generation wins). Off by default; exposed across Rust core + Python + TypeScript.

SUBJECTIVE (author's framing, from the PR body)
  Without this flag a plain scan over an LSM-spec table returns stale data — it never sees
  rows written via the LSM merge_insert path until an external compaction folds them into the
  base. The flag is positioned as opt-in, behavior-preserving by default, with unsupported
  query shapes rejected as hard errors (not silent fallback) because the caller opted in.

OBJECTIVE (what I checked, tied to ground truth)
  Net diff read in full: 16/16 files, 0 omitted. New module rust/lancedb/src/table/query/lsm.rs
  (+441) and merge.rs test block (+349) carry the weight; bindings + docs are mechanical.

  Dispatch wiring
  • rust/lancedb/src/table/query.rs:90 — `if query.base.use_lsm_read { return lsm::create_lsm_plan(...) }`
    placed before the standard scan setup. Single entry point; clean.
  • rust/lancedb/src/query.rs:781/809 — `QueryRequest.use_lsm_read: bool` added with
    `Default = false`; builder `use_lsm_read()` on the `QueryBase` blanket impl. Default path
    unchanged — confirmed the field defaults false in the Default impl (query.rs:809).

  Guard rails (lsm.rs)
  • create_lsm_plan:54 — no MemWAL index details ⇒ Error::InvalidInput with an actionable
    message. Verified by test lsm_read_without_spec_errors (merge.rs) + Python/TS equivalents.
  • reject_unsupported:99 — multi-vector, hybrid, with_row_id, reranker, order_by,
    dynamic/expr projection, and Substrait filters all return Error::NotSupported. Caller
    opted in, so hard error not silent base-only fallback — matches the body's stated contract.
  • remote/table.rs:819 — remote (Cloud) tables reject use_lsm_read with NotSupported for both
    AnyQuery arms. Covered by TS test "useLsmRead errors without an LSM spec" (local) but the
    remote-rejection arm itself is NOT covered by a test.
  • pk_columns:142 — MemWAL index but empty unenforced PK ⇒ Error::Runtime. Not covered by a
    test (no fixture builds that state), but it is a defensive guard, not a happy path.

  Read context assembly (build_read_context:159)
  • Uses `dataset.object_store(None)` deliberately rather than ObjectStore::from_uri, with an
    inline WHY comment: from_uri builds a fresh registry that would miss memory:// and
    custom-registered stores. base_path = branch_location().path matches
    list_mem_wal_latest_shard_ids. This is the correct-store subtlety and it is documented.
  • The active shard is overridden by the cached ShardWriter's authoritative in-memory manifest
    + memtables (merge/lsm.rs read_snapshot:300). Snapshot-after-close still sees flushed
    generations via the on-disk manifest — covered by lsm_read_after_close_sees_flushed.

  Pagination
  • plain_plan:264 calls LsmScanner::limit(limit, offset) (applies both internally); offset-only
    (no limit) is folded via apply_limit_offset. fts_plan/vector_plan compute k = limit+offset
    then slice with GlobalLimitExec afterward — correct top-k-then-page. No double application
    spotted.

  Vector arm (vector_plan:301, resolve_distance_type:380, to_fixed_size_list:420)
  • Distance type: explicit query metric → column's vector-index metric → L2 fallback, so
    cross-source distances match the maintained memtable index. Sensible.
  • Binary (uint8) FSL query vectors are explicitly rejected (NotSupported); non-f32 numeric
    rejected as InvalidInput. Documented as a deliberate scope cut (body: binary-vector LSM
    search is follow-up).

  Tests (the strong part of this PR)
  • Rust merge.rs: active-memtable visibility, dedup-newest-wins, point-lookup filter,
    multi-shard (bucket spec), flushed-after-close, error-without-spec, FTS (memtable-only term
    + base term), vector KNN (memtable row ranks first). Genuinely exercises the new paths
    against real LsmScanner output, not mocks.
  • Python test_merge_insert_lsm.py: sync active-memtable, dedup, error-without-spec, async.
  • nodejs table.test.ts: useLsmRead surfaces memtable row; errors without spec.

  Bindings parity
  • Python query.py: field carried in all three concrete sync to_query_object (vector:1388,
    fts:1591, empty:1662). Verified at head that the base builder to_query_object (1126) is
    abstract and the hybrid one (1764) raises NotImplementedError — so no concrete sync builder
    silently drops the flag. async/take builders forward to the Rust inner via _inner.use_lsm_read().
  • from_inner:531 normalizes default False → None so a round-tripped Query only carries the
    flag when explicitly set. _sync_query_to_async (table.py:4953) propagates it.
  • TS: useLsmRead() on StandardQueryBase; napi bindings on Query/VectorQuery/TakeQuery.

ASSESSMENT
  risk: med — refined from diff, not raised above the path-only baseline. This is a read-path
    feature gated behind an opt-in flag that defaults false; the standard scan path is
    untouched (single early-return at query.rs:90, Default::use_lsm_read = false). No unsafe, no
    format-write/serialization changes, no wire-compat break (additive bool field). The new
    code is substantial but well-fenced and well-tested. I do NOT see grounds for high.
  urgency: high (baseline) — core library + public API.
  escalation: forced on [public-api, large-blast-radius] → full rounds regardless of how clean
    it reads. Routing, not a risk verdict.
  could not read: none (0 omitted). I did NOT download CI logs to attribute the failing job.

  Merge-readiness (separate from code quality): merge_state is ci=red, mergeable=false. The
  head commit's "Linux (NodeJS 20)" check is a failure and "Linux (NodeJS 18)" was cancelled.
  These are hard merge gates independent of review judgment.

DRAFT COMMENTS
  issue(blocking): CI is red on the head commit — "Linux (NodeJS 20)" failed. Resolve the
    failing NodeJS job (and the not-mergeable / needs-rebase state) before merge.
    acceptance: head commit shows all required checks green and the PR is mergeable.

  issue(non-blocking): The remote-table rejection (remote/table.rs:819) has no test. A small
    test asserting a RemoteTable query with use_lsm_read returns NotSupported would lock the
    Cloud contract so a future refactor can't silently start sending the flag to the server.
    acceptance: a test covers the remote NotSupported arm (author's call; not gating).

  suggestion: pk_columns() returning Error::Runtime for "MemWAL index but empty unenforced PK"
    is currently unexercised. If that state is reachable in practice, a one-line test would
    pin the message; if it's structurally impossible, a comment saying so would help the next
    reader. Author's call.

  question: For the sync hybrid builder, calling .use_lsm_read() then running a hybrid query —
    the flag is dropped (hybrid to_query_object raises; to_arrow builds sub-builders without
    propagating _use_lsm_read). The Rust path rejects hybrid+LSM anyway, so the user gets an
    error either way. Is the intent that hybrid+use_lsm_read surfaces a clear NotSupported
    rather than silently ignoring the flag? Not gating, but worth confirming the UX.

RECOMMENDATION: block on 1 condition (CI green + mergeable). The code itself reads as approvable
  — coherent design, correct store handling, hard-error-on-unsupported contract honored, and
  strong multi-language test coverage — but it cannot merge while the head commit is CI-red and
  not mergeable, and escalation forces full rounds.
  confidence: high on the design read and test coverage; medium on the CI failure's root cause
    (I did not pull the NodeJS job logs to confirm whether it is this PR's code or flaky/infra).
  what would make this wrong: if the NodeJS failure is a genuine binding/compile error from
    this PR's napi additions (nodejs/src/query.rs), the "code is approvable" read is too
    generous and there is a second blocking code condition hiding in the red CI.
